meta.title: SG-SCD-3: Embedded Apps, Data Actions, and Query Governance

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which approach is used for client-side embedded Sigma analytics?

options.text: Materialize every workbook page to a Cloud Storage bucket before embedding
options.explanation: Sigma embedding is based on generating a signed URL (or JWT-based token) server-side with appropriate claims such as user email, team, and user attributes, then rendering it in an iframe within the host application.

options.text: Export the workbook to a static HTML file and host it on the customer's CDN
options.explanation: Sigma workbooks are dynamic, live-querying documents; they cannot be meaningfully exported to static HTML while retaining interactive filtering and real-time data.

options.text: Generate a signed embed URL or JWT on the application server and load it in an iframe
options.explanation: Materialization is an optional performance optimization for caching expensive queries; it is not required before embedding and does not produce an embeddable artifact.

options.text: Copy the workbook data into the embedding application's own database first
options.explanation: Sigma always queries the connected warehouse at query time; data is not copied into the embedding application's database.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. why is granular sharing generally safer than granting broad workspace access to everyone?

options.text: It disables embedding risks
options.explanation: is correct here because granular sharing supports least-privilege access. **B** is incorrect because cost is a separate concern. **C** is incorrect because sharing does not automatically replace data filtering. **D** is incorrect because embedding still requires secure design. [Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

options.text: It limits access to only the content a user actually needs
It guarantees lower warehouse cost
It replaces row-level filtering automatically

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. a team wants one date selector to filter multiple workbook elements at once. What is the best way to they configure?

options.text: A control target
options.explanation: is correct here because controls can target one or more workbook elements for shared filtering. **B** is incorrect because warehouse roles govern source access. **C** is incorrect because materialization caches results rather than wiring interactions. **D** is incorrect because JWT claims are for embedding contexts. [Intro to control elements](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/intro-to-control-elements)

options.text: A materialization policy
A warehouse role
A JWT claim set

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which warehouse platforms can Sigma commonly connect to?

options.text: Only local CSV files
options.explanation: is correct here because Sigma commonly connects to warehouse and database platforms such as Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, and Databricks. **B** is incorrect because it is not limited to local CSVs or Power BI models. **C** is incorrect because external systems are core to Sigma. [Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

options.text: No external systems
Only Power BI semantic models
Cloud data warehouses such as Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, and Databricks

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. why should a Sigma developer avoid unnecessary cross-joins in workbook modeling?

options.text: Cross-joins multiply row counts between tables, producing extremely large intermediate result sets and expensive warehouse queries
options.explanation: Sigma's visual join builder supports multiple join types; while cross-joins may require specific configuration, they are not entirely unsupported in Sigma.

options.text: Cross-joins disable workbook controls from filtering the joined elements
options.explanation: The presence of a cross-join does not prevent materialization from being configured; materialization can persist any query result, including expensive ones.

options.text: Cross-joins are not supported in Sigma's visual join builder and must be written in raw SQL
options.explanation: Workbook controls and filters operate on the output of the query regardless of join type; cross-joins do not disable control filtering.

options.text: Cross-joins prevent materialization from being scheduled on the affected workbook
options.explanation: A cross-join produces the Cartesian product of two tables — a table with M rows joined to one with N rows produces M × N output rows. For large tables this creates massive intermediate result sets that consume significant warehouse compute, memory, and cost, often unintentionally.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. a workbook is slow because a massive join happens before any filters are applied. What is the best first optimization?

options.text: Apply the filter before the join to reduce row volume entering the join operation
options.explanation: Materialization caches the result of the existing expensive query; it does not restructure the query to avoid the expensive join. It can help for repeated reads but does not fix the underlying inefficiency.

options.text: Convert the join to a published dataset to reduce per-workbook join overhead
options.explanation: Splitting the join across workbook pages does not change the SQL generated for any individual element; each page would still join the full dataset.

options.text: Enable materialization with a daily schedule so the joined data is pre-cached
options.explanation: Publishing to a dataset moves the join definition but does not add a filter; the same expensive full-scan join would still execute at query time.

options.text: Split the join into multiple workbook pages to distribute the load
options.explanation: Reducing the number of rows before the join significantly lowers the amount of data the warehouse must process. Applying selective filters early is the most impactful first step because it directly shrinks join input size and enables better pushdown.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. when is materialization most useful in Sigma?

options.text: When you need to manage workspace permissions
options.explanation: is correct here because materialization can persist expensive query results to improve responsiveness. **B** is incorrect because Sigma still depends on the warehouse. **C** is incorrect because permissions and JWT generation are separate concerns. **D** is incorrect because not applicable. [Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

options.text: When you want to remove all warehouse dependencies
When you need to generate JWTs
When repeated workbook queries are expensive and a cached warehouse table will improve performance

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. what is the main purpose of user attributes in Sigma security design?

options.text: To support dynamic row-level filtering and tenant personalization based on user identity
options.explanation: Warehouse credentials are configured in Connections by Admins and applied at the connection level, not per user via user attributes.

options.text: To configure the appearance and branding of the Sigma interface per team
options.explanation: Sigma's branding and theming is controlled in Organization settings, not through user attributes.

options.text: To define the warehouse connection credentials for each individual user
options.explanation: User attributes are key-value pairs assigned to users or teams that can be referenced in dataset filters and embed parameters, enabling dynamic row-level security so each user only sees the data relevant to their identity or tenant.

options.text: To set rate limits on API requests made by each user account
options.explanation: API rate limits are not configured through user attributes; they are governed by Sigma's platform policies and the REST API authentication layer.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which calculation type is best for running totals or rankings within Sigma?

options.text: JWT claims
options.explanation: is correct here because running totals and rankings are window-style analytical calculations. **B** is incorrect because JWTs, credentials, and roles are unrelated to analytic formulas. **C** is incorrect because not applicable. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: Workspace roles
Connection credentials
Window functions such as running average or rank

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. what is the difference between workspace permissions and workbook permissions?

options.text: Workbook permissions grant broader organization-wide access while workspace permissions are scoped to a single asset
options.explanation: Workspace permissions control access to all content types within the workspace, not just scheduled exports.

options.text: They are always identical because workbook permissions inherit from the workspace and cannot be overridden
options.explanation: Permissions can be set independently at the workbook level and can differ from the workspace-level grant; they are not always identical.

options.text: Workspace permissions apply only to scheduled exports while workbook permissions apply to interactive viewing
options.explanation: This reverses the relationship — workspace permissions are broader (covering many assets), while workbook permissions are scoped to a single asset.

options.text: Workspace permissions grant access across all content in the workspace, while workbook permissions target specific workbooks with potentially different access levels
options.explanation: Workspace-level permissions apply broadly to all content inside that workspace, while individual workbook permissions allow more granular access control — for example, granting one team Viewer access at the workspace level but Editor access on a specific workbook.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. what can the Sigma REST API help automate?

options.text: Warehouse partition pruning
options.explanation: is correct here because the API supports programmatic management tasks such as exports and content operations. **B** is incorrect because warehouse pruning happens in the source platform. **C** is incorrect because font rendering and notebook kernels are unrelated. [Sigma API documentation](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/api-reference)

options.text: Notebook kernel restarts
Workbook management and exports
Browser font rendering

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. a developer needs a toggle that switches a chart between Gross Margin and Revenue. Which Sigma feature is the most appropriate fit in this scenario?

options.text: An input table
options.explanation: is correct here because controls are the right interactive mechanism for switching parameters or views. **B** is incorrect because input tables store writeback data. **C** is incorrect because roles and secrets are unrelated to interactivity. [Intro to control elements](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/intro-to-control-elements)

options.text: A workspace role
A connection secret
A workbook control tied to the chart logic

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. why might a team tag workbook versions before a release?

options.text: Version tags mark known-good milestones that can be referenced for rollback, audit, or embedding specific published states
options.explanation: Version tags capture a snapshot of the workbook definition, not the underlying data; they do not trigger materialization or persist query results.

options.text: Version tags copy the workbook to a separate workspace for QA testing
options.explanation: Version tags create named checkpoints in a workbook's history. Teams can tag a version before deploying to ensure they can roll back to a known-good state, and embedded analytics can reference a tagged version to pin the embed to a stable workbook release.

options.text: Version tags enable embed tokens to be generated without a signing secret
options.explanation: Embed tokens always require a valid signing secret; version tags have no bearing on that security requirement.

options.text: Version tags automatically materialize the workbook's data at the point of tagging
options.explanation: Version tags do not copy workbooks to another workspace; they are metadata applied to the workbook's history within its current location.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which TWO workbook design choices most appropriate support a polished analyst experience? (Select TWO)

options.text: Avoid datasets so each page starts from scratch
options.explanation: are correct because clear organization and purposeful controls improve usability and maintainability. **C** is incorrect because permanently hiding needed filters hurts usability. **D** is incorrect because avoiding datasets reduces reuse and consistency. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: Organize related pages and reusable navigation clearly
options.explanation: are correct because clear organization and purposeful controls improve usability and maintainability. **C** is incorrect because permanently hiding needed filters hurts usability. **D** is incorrect because avoiding datasets reduces reuse and consistency. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: Hide all filters permanently even when needed
Use controls that target relevant elements

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. a developer wants to troubleshoot why a workbook calculation is expensive. What is the most useful first step?

options.text: Disable permissions checks
options.explanation: is correct here because performance troubleshooting starts by understanding the query shape and reducing expensive operations. **B** is incorrect because API token rotation is unrelated. **C** is incorrect because permissions are not the root cause of query complexity. **D** is incorrect because moving workspaces alone rarely fixes the query plan. [Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

options.text: Rotate every API token
Inspect the generated query path and simplify filters, joins, or calculations that block efficient pushdown
Publish the workbook to a different workspace only

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. what is the main advantage of Sigma's visual join builder?

options.text: It encrypts source data
options.explanation: is correct here because the visual join builder makes join configuration approachable while still pushing SQL to the warehouse. **B** is incorrect because it does not force denormalization. **C** is incorrect because it is not an encryption feature. **D** is incorrect because permissions are managed separately. [Overview of joining data](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/joins-overview)

options.text: It lets users define join types and conditions without hand-writing SQL
It replaces workbook permissions
It physically denormalizes all tables automatically

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. an embedded workbook must apply a tenant-specific filter automatically for each signed-in customer. Which design best supports this?

options.text: Use a workbook control with a hardcoded list of tenant values that users select manually
options.explanation: Embedding context plus user attributes supports dynamic tenant-aware filtering at scale. The application server encodes the tenant identifier in the signed embed URL; Sigma maps it to a user attribute that drives a dataset filter, so each customer sees only their own data without requiring separate workbooks or materials.

options.text: Create one separate workbook per customer and generate a unique embed URL for each workbook
options.explanation: Maintaining one workbook per customer is operationally unscalable — any change to the workbook layout must be replicated across every customer copy.

options.text: Pass user or tenant context through signed embed parameters and apply user attributes to drive row-level filtering in the dataset
options.explanation: A hardcoded control list requires the customer to select their tenant, which is a security risk (they could select another tenant) and poor UX.

options.text: Materialize a separate warehouse table per tenant and point each embed at the appropriate table
options.explanation: Creating and managing a separate materialized table per tenant is highly operationally complex and does not scale as the customer base grows.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. which TWO practices most improve governed reuse in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: Publish shared datasets
options.explanation: are correct because shared datasets and reusable logic reduce duplication and improve consistency. **B** is incorrect because rebuilding joins everywhere increases drift and maintenance cost. **D** is incorrect because full admin rights are unnecessary and risky. [Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

options.text: Rebuild joins independently in every workbook
Centralize common calculations in reusable assets
options.explanation: are correct because shared datasets and reusable logic reduce duplication and improve consistency. **B** is incorrect because rebuilding joins everywhere increases drift and maintenance cost. **D** is incorrect because full admin rights are unnecessary and risky. [Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

options.text: Give every user full admin rights

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a workbook uses complex calculations across the same billions-row dataset every morning. Which feature is most likely to reduce repeated runtime cost in this scenario?

options.text: Add more workbook pages
options.explanation: is correct here because materialization can persist costly intermediate results as warehouse tables for reuse. **B** is incorrect because extra pages do not optimize queries. **C** is incorrect because free-text controls may worsen usability. **D** is incorrect because disabling cache is counterproductive. [Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

options.text: Disable the warehouse cache
Materialize the expensive result set
Turn every control into free text

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. what is the primary purpose of a Sigma workbook?

options.text: A low-level warehouse driver
options.explanation: is correct here because a Sigma workbook is the core collaborative surface for analysis and presentation. **B** is incorrect because warehouse drivers connect to data sources but are not the analytical document. **C** is incorrect because Sigma does not replace underlying warehouse storage. **D** is incorrect because it is not an OS package. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: A replacement for warehouse storage
An operating-system package
A collaborative analytical document containing pages and data elements

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which action best reduces workbook latency when users regularly open a page with a highly selective control?

options.text: Change all numbers to strings
options.explanation: is correct here because early selective filtering reduces processed data volume and improves performance. **B** is incorrect because extra visuals add cost. **C** is incorrect because datasets are usually beneficial. **D** is incorrect because string conversion is unrelated. [Intro to control elements](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/intro-to-control-elements)

options.text: Add unrelated visual elements
Apply the control-driven filter as early as possible
Disable datasets

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what does Sigma version history help project teams do in this scenario?

options.text: Rotate service-account credentials
options.explanation: is correct here because version history supports review and rollback of workbook changes. **B** is incorrect because it does not materialize data. **C** is incorrect because credential rotation is an admin task. **D** is incorrect because embed tokens are separate. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: Review and restore prior workbook states
Build embed tokens
Materialize warehouse tables automatically

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. why is query pushdown important in Sigma?

options.text: It copies every table into the browser
options.explanation: is correct here because pushdown uses the warehouse engine efficiently. **B** is incorrect because Sigma still generates SQL. **C** is incorrect because tables are not copied wholesale into the browser. **D** is incorrect because joins remain relevant. [Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

options.text: It removes the need for joins
It lets the warehouse do the heavy computation close to the data
It avoids all SQL generation

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which option describes the difference between workspace permissions and workbook permissions in this scenario?

options.text: Workspace permissions apply only to API tokens
options.explanation: is correct here because workspace permissions are broader and workbook permissions are more granular. **B** is incorrect because the two scopes are not identical. **C** is incorrect because workbook permissions do not administer the organization. **D** is incorrect because workspace permissions are not limited to APIs. [Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

options.text: Workspace permissions can grant broader access across content, while workbook permissions target specific assets
Workbook permissions can administer the whole org
They are always identical

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. which TWO capabilities make input tables useful for planning workflows? (Select TWO)

options.text: Manual governed data entry
options.explanation: are correct because input tables support controlled manual entry and writeback patterns for planning or adjustments. **C** is incorrect because JWT generation is unrelated. **D** is incorrect because deleting history is not a feature goal of input tables. [Input tables overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/input-tables)

options.text: Writeback to the warehouse
options.explanation: are correct because input tables support controlled manual entry and writeback patterns for planning or adjustments. **C** is incorrect because JWT generation is unrelated. **D** is incorrect because deleting history is not a feature goal of input tables. [Input tables overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/input-tables)

options.text: Automatic JWT generation
Version-history deletion

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. why should a developer prefer a published dataset over rebuilding the same joins in many workbooks?

options.text: Published datasets remove the need for a warehouse connection and can run offline
options.explanation: Sigma always queries the connected warehouse; published datasets do not bypass the warehouse or enable offline execution.

options.text: Published datasets grant every user who accesses them full Admin permissions on the source tables
options.explanation: Defining joins, column aliases, and metrics once in a published dataset means every workbook that builds on it shares the same business logic. If the definition changes — such as a revenue metric formula update — it propagates to all dependent workbooks automatically.

options.text: Published datasets automatically materialize their joins on a daily schedule
options.explanation: Materialization is a separate optional feature that must be explicitly configured; publishing a dataset does not automatically schedule materialization.

options.text: A published dataset centralizes business logic, improving governance and ensuring consistent metric definitions across all workbooks
options.explanation: Publishing a dataset does not grant users elevated warehouse permissions; access is still governed by Sigma workspace and workbook permissions.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what is the purpose of an embed sandbox or test environment?

options.text: To validate the embedded experience — including token generation, user attribute filtering, and layout — before rolling out to production users
options.explanation: A sandbox or test environment lets developers verify that embed token signing, user attribute injection, dynamic filtering, and iframe rendering all work correctly before exposing the integration to real end users in production.

options.text: To permanently replace the production embed with a read-only view that prevents data changes
options.explanation: A test environment is a temporary validation context, not a permanent production replacement; it does not alter data permissions.

options.text: To convert the embedded workbook into a standalone HTML export for offline distribution
options.explanation: Signed embed tokens must always be generated server-side using the embed client secret; a sandbox does not bypass this requirement.

options.text: To automatically generate a signed embed token without requiring a server-side application
options.explanation: Sigma embeds are live, interactive experiences that query the warehouse in real time; they cannot be converted to static HTML exports.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. which TWO statements about embedding Sigma are accurate? (Select TWO)

options.text: Embed parameters can pass dynamic context such as tenant filters
options.explanation: are correct because signed embed authorization and dynamic parameters are common embedded analytics patterns in Sigma. **C** is incorrect because materialization is optional and performance-related, not mandatory for embedding. **D** is incorrect because embedding still requires correct permission and security design. [Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

options.text: Embedding removes the need for permission design
options.explanation: are correct because signed embed authorization and dynamic parameters are common embedded analytics patterns in Sigma. **C** is incorrect because materialization is optional and performance-related, not mandatory for embedding. **D** is incorrect because embedding still requires correct permission and security design. [Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

options.text: Signed JWTs can authorize embedded access
Embedding requires materialization of every workbook

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a developer wants a toggle that switches a chart between Gross Margin and Revenue. Which Sigma feature is the best fit?

options.text: An input table where the user types the desired metric name each time
options.explanation: Having two columns in a published dataset does not provide the interactive toggle; the user would see both columns simultaneously rather than switching between them.

options.text: A published dataset with two separate metric columns pre-computed
options.explanation: Controls such as a list, radio button, or segmented button can be configured to drive chart logic through a conditional calculation — for example, `If([Control] = "Gross Margin", [Gross Margin], [Revenue])` — allowing the user to toggle the displayed metric interactively.

options.text: A workbook control — such as a segmented button — whose value drives a conditional calculation in the chart
options.explanation: Materialization schedules cache static snapshots; they cannot alternate between different metric views based on user interaction.

options.text: A materialization schedule that alternates between two pre-cached versions of the chart
options.explanation: Requiring users to type a metric name in an input table is error-prone and a poor UX pattern compared to a dedicated interactive control.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. which function family is best for replacing nulls with fallback values?

options.text: Workspace functions
options.explanation: is correct here because functions like `Coalesce` and `IsNull` handle null-related logic. **B** is incorrect because the other choices are not Sigma calculation families. **C** is incorrect because not applicable. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: JWT expiry functions
Embedding functions
Conditional functions such as `Coalesce` or `IsNull`

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. why might a project team tag workbook versions before a release in this scenario?

options.text: To hide datasets
options.explanation: is correct here because tagging versions helps release management and rollback. **B** is incorrect because JWT signing is separate. **C** is incorrect because input tables and datasets are different features. **D** is incorrect because not applicable. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: To create input tables
To enable JWT signing
To mark known-good milestones for rollback or audit

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. which TWO controls are most relevant when securing multi-tenant analytics in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: User attributes mapped to tenant identifiers for dynamic row-level filtering
options.explanation: User attributes enable dynamic row-level security — each embedded or logged-in user automatically sees only the rows matching their tenant, enforced at query time by Sigma.

options.text: Workbook color themes applied per tenant for visual differentiation
options.explanation: Workspace and workbook permissions control which users and teams can view, edit, or manage each content asset, preventing one tenant's users from accessing another tenant's workbooks.

options.text: Granular workspace or workbook permissions that control which teams can access each asset
options.explanation: Color themes are cosmetic customizations; they do not enforce data isolation between tenants.

options.text: Scheduled materialization runs that pre-cache data per tenant each night
options.explanation: Materialization schedules optimize query performance; they do not provide any security boundary between tenants, as all tenants would read from the same cached table unless additional filtering is applied.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. which scenario is a good fit for an input table in this scenario?

options.text: A developer wants to define JWT claims
options.explanation: is correct here because input tables support governed manual writeback scenarios like planning or commentary. **B** is incorrect because JWT claims are for embedding. **C** is incorrect because Sigma uses permission and filtering controls rather than OLS terminology here. **D** is incorrect because secret rotation is an admin operation. [Input tables overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/input-tables)

options.text: A sales manager manually enters quarterly targets that should be stored back in the warehouse
An admin wants to rotate OAuth secrets
An analyst wants to hide a column with OLS

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. what should be rotated periodically for connections that use service-account credentials?

options.text: The credentials or secrets for the service account
options.explanation: is correct here because credential rotation is a standard operational security practice. **B** is incorrect because tabs, rows, and controls are unrelated to secret hygiene. **C** is incorrect because not applicable. [Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

options.text: Input-table rows
Date controls
Workbook tabs

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. which element type is most appropriate for showing one headline KPI such as month-to-date revenue?

options.text: An API client
options.explanation: is correct here because KPI cards summarize a single metric clearly. **B** is incorrect because input tables capture writeback data. **C** is incorrect because join builders define data relationships. **D** is incorrect because API clients are not workbook elements. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: A KPI card
A join builder
An input table

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. why might a project team publish a Sigma dataset in this scenario?

options.text: To bypass the warehouse completely
options.explanation: is correct here because published datasets support reuse and governance across workbooks. **B** is incorrect because Sigma still queries the warehouse. **C** is incorrect because joins remain available. **D** is incorrect because datasets are not the same as APIs. [Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

options.text: To disable joins
To create a reusable governed semantic layer for many workbooks
To convert every workbook into an API

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. which TWO steps are most likely to improve a slow Sigma workbook? (Select TWO)

options.text: Filter data before large joins
options.explanation: are correct because early filtering and selective materialization are common performance improvements. **C** is incorrect because controls can be useful and disabling them blindly does not address root query cost. **D** is incorrect because folder duplication does not optimize execution. [Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

options.text: Duplicate the workbook into more folders
options.explanation: are correct because early filtering and selective materialization are common performance improvements. **C** is incorrect because controls can be useful and disabling them blindly does not address root query cost. **D** is incorrect because folder duplication does not optimize execution. [Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

options.text: Disable all controls
Materialize expensive repeated query results

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. what is the main tradeoff of materialization?

options.text: Automatic propagation of updated permissions from the source table to the materialized result
options.explanation: Materialization is by definition not real-time; data is only as fresh as the last completed refresh run, so freshness and zero maintenance cannot both be guaranteed.

options.text: Elimination of all warehouse costs because the data no longer queries the source
options.explanation: Materialized results improve query speed and reduce compute costs for repeated reads, but they introduce a staleness window — the cached table reflects the data as of the last scheduled refresh, not the current warehouse state.

options.text: Perfect real-time freshness with zero maintenance overhead
options.explanation: Warehouse costs are not eliminated — the materialization job itself consumes warehouse compute when it runs, and the cached table occupies warehouse storage.

options.text: Faster repeated reads at the cost of managing refresh schedules and accepting some data staleness between runs
options.explanation: Materialized tables are separate physical objects; permission changes on the source table are not automatically reflected in the materialized result without explicit reconfiguration.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a user should be able to explore and build ad hoc analyses but not administer the entire organization. Which Sigma role is the closest fit?

options.text: Viewer, because Viewers can explore workbooks and create new ones in their personal space
options.explanation: The Viewer role is focused on consuming published content; Viewers typically cannot create or edit workbooks, which makes it too restrictive for ad hoc analysis.

options.text: There is no built-in role for this; a custom role must be created via the API
options.explanation: The Creator and Explorer account types allow users to build analyses, create workbooks, and explore data without having the organization-wide administrative capabilities of the Admin role.

options.text: Creator or Explorer, because these roles support analysis and content authoring without organization-wide administration
options.explanation: Admin is the most privileged role, with access to organization settings, connections, and user management — far broader than what an analyst needs.

options.text: Admin, because ad hoc analysis requires elevated data access permissions
options.explanation: Sigma provides built-in account types that cover this use case; a custom API-created role is not required.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. why should embed tokens include an expiry?

options.text: To enable input tables
options.explanation: is correct here because expiration reduces risk from leaked or reused embed tokens. **B** is incorrect because it does not affect chart aesthetics. **C** is incorrect because input tables and datasets are separate features. **D** is incorrect because not applicable. [Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

options.text: To improve chart colors
To create datasets automatically
To limit the window in which the embedded authorization can be misused

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which Sigma feature supports manual business-user writeback to the connected warehouse?

options.text: Input tables
options.explanation: Input tables are designed specifically for governed manual data entry and writeback use cases, allowing business users to type values that are persisted back to the warehouse.

options.text: Version-tagged workbook templates
options.explanation: Workbook controls — such as dropdowns or date pickers — filter or parameterize the analytical view; they do not write new records to the warehouse.

options.text: Workbook controls with a list-value source
options.explanation: Version tags track content milestones for rollback and audit, not for data writeback.

options.text: Scheduled materialization jobs
options.explanation: Materialization schedules cache existing query results as warehouse tables; they do not accept user-entered data.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. which Sigma function category is most appropriate for expressions such as `DateAdd` and `DateDiff`?

options.text: Date and time functions
options.explanation: `DateAdd` and `DateDiff` operate on date and time values — adding intervals or computing the difference between two dates — which places them squarely in the date and time function category.

options.text: Conditional functions
options.explanation: Lookup functions retrieve matching values from another element or dataset based on a key; they do not manipulate date values.

options.text: Aggregate functions
options.explanation: Aggregate functions like `Sum`, `Count`, or `Avg` reduce many rows to a single value; date arithmetic operates at the row level.

options.text: Lookup and relationship functions
options.explanation: Conditional functions like `If` and `Coalesce` evaluate logical expressions; `DateAdd` and `DateDiff` are not conditional in nature.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. which role is usually most restricted in Sigma?

options.text: Admin
options.explanation: Creators have broader capabilities than Viewers, including the ability to build and publish workbooks.

options.text: Viewer
options.explanation: Explorers can also explore and interact with data more deeply than Viewers, such as editing in their personal Explore space.

options.text: Explorer
options.explanation: The Viewer account type provides the most restricted access — Viewers can consume published workbooks and interact with controls but cannot create, edit, or save workbooks, making it the least privileged of the common user-facing account types.

options.text: Creator
options.explanation: Admin is the most privileged account type, with full control over organization settings, connections, and user management.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which Sigma capability is most useful when an application must export workbook results programmatically on a schedule?

options.text: User attributes that restrict which rows are included in the exported data
options.explanation: Version tags mark workbook definition milestones; they do not trigger or automate data exports.

options.text: Workbook version tags that snapshot the workbook definition at export time
options.explanation: User attributes control data visibility at query time; they are a security mechanism, not an export automation tool.

options.text: Materialization schedules that pre-cache the data and make it available for download
options.explanation: The Sigma REST API exposes export endpoints that allow an external application or scheduler to trigger workbook exports programmatically, in formats such as CSV, XLSX, or PDF, on a custom schedule.

options.text: The Sigma REST API, which supports programmatic scheduled exports of workbook content
options.explanation: Materialization pre-caches query results as warehouse tables; the cached data is not automatically pushed or exported to an external destination.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. why might a developer link workbooks together?

options.text: To synchronize version history across both workbooks
options.explanation: Workbook links allow a developer to pass filter values or parameters from one workbook to another and create drill-through navigation paths, giving users a connected analytical experience across separate content.

options.text: To grant the target workbook access to the source workbook's warehouse connection
options.explanation: Linking workbooks does not merge them or create shared datasets; the two workbooks remain independent and manage their own data sources.

options.text: To merge the two workbooks into one shared dataset automatically
options.explanation: Version history is tracked independently per workbook; linking does not synchronize versions between them.

options.text: To reuse context parameters and enable navigation across related analytical experiences
options.explanation: Warehouse connections are managed in Administration and are not shared or granted through workbook links.
